steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Biloma, bile leak, hepatic abscess, or biliary complication related to the existing right-sided drain; also possible choledocholithiasis or biliary obstruction given history of gallstone pancreatitis and prior ERCP/sphincterectomy
ensemble.information_gap: The anatomic source and nature of the RUQ pathology — specifically whether there is a biliary complication (bile leak, biloma, abscess, or ductal obstruction) related to the existing drain or biliary tree, versus an alternative intra-abdominal process — cannot be determined from history, exam, and labs alone
ensemble.expected_finding: A peri-hepatic or intra-abdominal fluid collection (biloma or abscess), biliary ductal dilation, or evidence of bile leak around the existing drain; alternatively, cholecystitis if the gallbladder is still present, or another intra-abdominal inflammatory process
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient has a right-sided drain with bile and intermittent blood-tinged output, an elevated WBC (14.4 K/uL) with neutrophilia (87.9%), mildly elevated bilirubin (1.6 mg/dL), elevated INR (1.6), and three days of RUQ pain — CT with contrast is the appropriate next step to characterize the drain site, surrounding structures, and biliary anatomy without prior imaging available.
grounding: Physical examination: 'right-sided ____ drain ___ place, small amount of ecchymosis around drain, bile draining freely (intermittenly blood-tinged); purulent drainage subsided'
History: 'Gallstone pancreatitis s/p ERCP and sphincterectomy'
History: 'RUQ pain... three days prior to admission which persisted'
Laboratory: 'White Blood Cells: 14.4 K/uL (ref 4.0-11.0)'
Laboratory: 'Neutrophils: 87.9 % (ref 50.0-70.0)'
Laboratory: 'Bilirubin, Total: 1.6 mg/dL (ref 0.0-1.5)'
Laboratory: 'INR(PT): 1.6 (ref 0.9-1.1)'
Laboratory: 'Lipase: 25.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — pancreatitis less likely
Laboratory: 'ALT: 13.0, AST: 19.0' — hepatocellular injury not prominent
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has a right-sided drain with bile and intermittently blood-tinged output, leukocytosis with neutrophilia, mildly elevated bilirubin, and a coagulopathy (INR 1.6) in the setting of three days of RUQ pain — all pointing toward an ongoing biliary or peri-hepatic process rather than simple cholelithiasis. With no prior imaging available and a complex surgical history (ERCP, sphincterectomy, right hemicolectomy), CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the most efficient single test to define the anatomy around the drain, identify any fluid collection, abscess, or biloma, assess the biliary tree for obstruction, and survey for other intra-abdominal pathology.

With no prior imaging available and a complex surgical history (ERCP, sphincterectomy, right hemicolectomy), CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the most efficient single test to define the anatomy around the drain, identify any fluid collection, abscess, or biloma, assess the biliary tree for obstruction, and survey for other intra-abdominal pathology.

verification.actual_finding: CT demonstrated a distended gallbladder with multiple gallstones including one at the neck, gallbladder wall thickening with mucosal hyperemia, adjacent fat stranding, and reactive duodenal wall edema, consistent with acute cholecystitis; no biliary ductal dilation, biloma, abscess, or free fluid was identified.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The result confirmed one of the explicitly anticipated alternatives — cholecystitis — thereby resolving the anatomic source of the RUQ pathology as the physician expected it might, while ruling out biloma, bile leak, and ductal obstruction.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction; bile leak given drain in place with intermittently blood-tinged and previously purulent drainage
ensemble.information_gap: The CT confirms acute cholecystitis with a stone at the gallbladder neck, but does not provide real-time assessment of gallbladder wall vascularity, pericholecystic fluid, common bile duct diameter, or presence of stones in the CBD — and the patient already has a drain in place suggesting a prior intervention whose adequacy needs reassessment with dynamic imaging.
ensemble.expected_finding: Sonographic Murphy's sign, pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder wall thickening with hyperemia on Doppler, and/or a dilated common bile duct with or without a visible CBD stone confirming acute cholecystitis and evaluating for ongoing biliary obstruction.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The CT already shows gallbladder wall thickening, mucosal hyperemia, fat stranding, and a stone at the gallbladder neck consistent with acute cholecystitis, and ultrasound adds real-time sonographic Murphy's sign, Doppler flow assessment, CBD measurement, and dynamic evaluation of the existing drain — all directly relevant to surgical planning and severity grading.
grounding: CT Gallbladder: 'The gallbladder is distended with multiple gallstones and a gallstone at the gallbladder neck (02:29). The gallbladder wall is thickened with mucosal hyperemia and adjacent fat stranding.'
Physical exam: 'right-sided ____ drain in place, small amount of ecchymosis around drain, bile draining freely (intermittently blood-tinged); purulent drainage subsided'
CT Liver: 'no intra or extrahepatic biliary duct dilatation'
Labs: 'White Blood Cells: 14.4 K/uL', 'Neutrophils: 87.9%', 'Bilirubin, Total: 1.6 mg/dL', 'INR(PT): 1.6'
History: 'Gallstone pancreatitis s/p ERCP and sphincterectomy', 'RUQ pain three days prior to admission'
ensemble.reasoning: The CT has already established acute cholecystitis with a neck stone, but I need ultrasound to confirm the sonographic Murphy's sign (which CT cannot provide), assess Doppler perfusion of the gallbladder wall to evaluate for gangrenous change, precisely measure the common bile duct for residual obstruction given her prior ERCP/sphincterotomy history, and evaluate the existing drain's position and surrounding fluid — all of which directly inform whether this patient needs urgent cholecystectomy, repeat biliary intervention, or drain repositioning.

representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The CT confirms acute cholecystitis with a stone at the gallbladder neck, but does not provide real-time assessment of gallbladder wall vascularity, pericholecystic fluid, common bile duct diameter, or presence of stones in the CBD — and the patient already has a drain in place suggesting a prior intervention whose adequacy needs reassessment with dynamic imaging.

verification.actual_finding: Ultrasound showed a distended gallbladder with sludge, stones, an impacted gallstone at the gallbladder neck, and marked wall thickening (1.0 cm) with mural edema, with a non-dilated CBD measuring 4 mm.
verification.relation_to_expectation: The report confirmed gallbladder wall thickening and an impacted neck stone consistent with acute cholecystitis, and addressed the CBD diameter question (not dilated, no obstruction), though Doppler hyperemia and sonographic Murphy's sign were not explicitly documented.
